programs.name: Centennial Care Community Benefit Program
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing or dressing, this program can provide home care services to keep them living independently.

localEntities.name: Managed Care Organizations (MCOs); ALTSD for waitlist

programs.intro:
If your aging parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or taking medication, Centennial Care Community Benefit provides home care services to help them stay in their own home. The program covers personal care aides, adult day health care, emergency response systems, home modifications, and skilled nursing visits.

Your parent must first qualify for New Mexico Medicaid and need assistance with at least 2 activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility, or transferring). Because this supplements natural family support rather than replacing it, the program won't provide 24-hour care or cover room and board costs.

The program operates through managed care organizations statewide. If your parent doesn't qualify for full Medicaid, they can join a waitlist through the Central Registry, with priority given based on need level.

summary: Age 65+ or any age if disabled
Must qualify for New Mexico Medicaid first
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need assistance with at least 2 Activities of Daily Living: bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility, or transferring between bed/chair
otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in a Centennial Care Managed Care Organization

applicationGuide.summary: If your parent has Medicaid and an MCO, contact their care coordinator for assessment.

steps.title: Check Medicaid status
steps.description: Your parent must have full New Mexico Medicaid coverage first. If they don't, apply for Medicaid through the state before proceeding.

steps.title: Enroll in managed care
steps.description: If your parent has Medicaid but no MCO enrollment, call 1-800-283-4465 to choose a managed care organization.

steps.title: Request assessment
steps.description: Contact your parent's MCO care coordinator to request a functional assessment for Community Benefit services.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: A clinician will assess whether your parent needs help with 2 or more activities of daily living to determine Nursing Facility Level of Care.

applicationGuide.waitlist: If your parent doesn't qualify for full Medicaid, contact ALTSD for Central Registry waitlist placement
applicationGuide.tip: You must start with Agency-Based services for 120 days before switching to Self-Directed care options

contentSections.text: Your parent must qualify for full New Mexico Medicaid first. This isn't a standalone program. If they don't have Medicaid, contact ALTSD to join the Central Registry waitlist.
The functional assessment looks at actual need for help, not just medical diagnoses. Having dementia doesn't automatically qualify—your parent must need hands-on assistance with daily activities.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for this program?
faqs.answer: Yes. This program helps people stay in their own homes and doesn't affect home ownership. The program specifically provides alternatives to nursing home care.

faqs.question: What if Dad has Medicaid but his income is too high for full coverage?
faqs.answer: He can still access services through the Central Registry waitlist managed by ALTSD. Priority is given based on need level, but specific wait times aren't published.

faqs.question: Will this program provide 24-hour care for Mom with dementia?
faqs.answer: No. The program supplements family and natural supports, not replace them. It won't cover 24-hour supervision or room and board costs. Consider it as additional help, not total care replacement.

faqs.question: Can we choose our own home care provider?
faqs.answer: Initially, no. You must use Agency-Based services for the first 120 days. After that, you can switch to Self-Directed care where you have more control over provider selection.

faqs.question: What happens if Dad's care needs increase?
faqs.answer: Your MCO care coordinator will reassess his needs and adjust services accordingly. The program covers a range from basic personal care to skilled nursing visits based on individual requirements.

faqs.question: Does this cover home modifications like ramps or grab bars?
faqs.answer: Yes. Environmental modifications are covered services, but they're based on individual assessment and need. Your care coordinator will determine what modifications are appropriate and covered.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: Complete medical care and daily support services under one program for seniors who need nursing home-level care but want to stay home.

localEntities.name: InnovAge New Mexico PACE, 904 Las Lomas Rd NE, Albuquerque, NM 87102 (opened 1998)[7]

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management, PACE provides all their medical care and support services under one program. Because PACE coordinates everything — from doctor visits to transportation to meals — your parent won't need to juggle multiple providers or insurance approvals.

PACE covers primary care, hospital care, emergency services, physical therapy, prescription drugs, dental care, meals, social services, and transportation to appointments. Most participants pay nothing out of pocket if they have Medicaid. The catch: PACE is only available in Albuquerque, and once enrolled, your parent must use PACE providers exclusively for all covered services.

Your parent must be certified by the state as needing nursing home-level care but be able to live safely in the community with PACE support.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by the state as requiring nursing home level of care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other essential activities)
otherRequirements: Must live in the PACE service area (Albuquerque only)
Must be eligible for Medicare, Medicaid, or both
Must be capable of living safely in the community with PACE support

applicationGuide.summary: Call InnovAge directly to start the application and assessment process.

steps.title: Call InnovAge
steps.description: Contact InnovAge at (844) 945-0467 to discuss your parent's needs and schedule an initial assessment.

steps.title: Complete assessment
steps.description: InnovAge will evaluate whether your parent meets the nursing home level of care requirement and can live safely in the community.

steps.title: Verify eligibility
steps.description: Confirm Medicare or Medicaid eligibility and residence in the Albuquerque service area.

steps.title: Enroll
steps.description: If approved, your parent will transition all covered medical care to PACE providers.

applicationGuide.tip: Call even if you're unsure about eligibility — InnovAge can help determine if your parent qualifies for the nursing home level of care certification.

offices.name: InnovAge New Mexico PACE
offices.address: 904 Las Lomas Rd NE, Albuquerque, NM 87102

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in Albuquerque. If your parent lives outside this service area, they cannot access the program.

Once enrolled, PACE becomes your parent's sole provider for all covered services — they cannot use other doctors or hospitals for covered care. However, they can withdraw from the program at any time.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep seeing her current doctor if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE becomes the sole provider of all covered medical services once enrolled. Your mom would need to switch to PACE physicians and use PACE facilities for all care.

faqs.question: What if we live in Las Cruces or Santa Fe?
faqs.answer: PACE is only available in Albuquerque. Families outside this service area cannot access the program in New Mexico.

faqs.question: How much will Dad pay each month?
faqs.answer: If your dad has Medicaid, he pays no monthly premium. If he only has Medicare, he may pay a monthly premium for additional coverage. There are no deductibles or co-pays in the program.

faqs.question: What if Dad needs nursing home care while in PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing home care when needed. Because the program already determined your dad needs nursing home-level care, they coordinate all aspects of his care whether he's at home or in a facility.

faqs.question: Can we try PACE and leave if it doesn't work?
faqs.answer: Yes. Participants can withdraw from the program at any time and return to traditional Medicare or Medicaid coverage.

faqs.question: How do we get the state certification for nursing home level of care?
faqs.answer: InnovAge will help coordinate this assessment as part of the enrollment process. The state evaluates whether your parent needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management.

programs.name: Community Benefit Program
programs.tagline: Free home care services to help your parent avoid nursing home placement while staying on Medicaid.

localEntities.name: Managed Care Organizations (MCOs) statewide; Aging & Long-Term Services Department for registry

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, New Mexico's Community Benefit Program provides free home care services to help them avoid nursing home placement. Because the program covers personal care, home health aide visits, adult day programs, and assisted living support based on your parent's individual needs, they can remain at home while getting the care they require.

Your parent must qualify for full Medicaid coverage first — which means income under $1,000/month for a single person and assets under $2,000. The program requires a clinical assessment proving your parent needs Nursing Facility Level of Care (daily assistance with 2+ activities like bathing, dressing, eating, or mobility). Services are coordinated through Medicaid Managed Care Organizations, with each care plan tailored to your parent's specific functional needs.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care — daily assistance with 2+ activities of daily living like bathing, dressing, eating, or mobility
otherRequirements: Enrolled in Medicaid Managed Care Organization

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your parent's Medicaid MCO care coordinator for assessment, or call 1-800-283-4465 if not enrolled in an MCO.

steps.title: Check Medicaid MCO enrollment
steps.description: If your parent has Medicaid but isn't enrolled in a Managed Care Organization, call 1-800-283-4465 first

steps.title: Contact MCO care coordinator
steps.description: Call your parent's MCO directly to request Community Benefit Program assessment

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: MCO will assess whether your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements

steps.title: Receive individualized care plan
steps.description: If eligible, MCO creates specific service plan based on your parent's needs

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified; depends on MCO assessment timeline
applicationGuide.waitlist: Central Registry waitlist exists for non-Medicaid eligible slots with limited positions
applicationGuide.tip: New participants must try Agency-Based Community Benefit for 120 days before switching to Self-Directed options

contentSections.heading: Medicaid Income Limits (2022 figures)
contentSections.footnote: 2022 Medicaid ABD limits; verify current amounts when applying

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (if your parent intends to return)
Burial plots and prepaid funeral contracts

tiers.name: Full Medicaid Route
tiers.description: Standard pathway for those meeting Medicaid income and asset limits
tiers.coverage: Full services via MCO care coordinator

tiers.name: Central Registry Route
tiers.description: Limited slots for those who don't qualify for full Medicaid
tiers.coverage: Based on need with limited open positions

categories.name: Identity
items: Proof of citizenship or legal alien status

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing functional limitations
Doctor's assessment of daily care needs

contentSections.text: Limited slots available through Central Registry for non-Medicaid eligible applicants. High demand means potential waitlist.
Must qualify for full Medicaid first. If your parent's income is slightly over the limit, consult with an elder law attorney about spend-down strategies.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is exempt from asset limits as long as your parent intends to return to it, regardless of current value.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: They may qualify for the Central Registry route with limited slots, or explore Medicaid spend-down options. Contact 1-800-432-2080 for Central Registry information.

faqs.question: How much control does my parent have over their care?
faqs.answer: New participants must use Agency-Based Community Benefit for 120 days first. After that, they can switch to Self-Directed Community Benefit for more control over services and providers.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's condition gets worse?
faqs.answer: The MCO will reassess their care needs and can adjust services accordingly. Some members with continuous needs may not require annual reassessment.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to be enrolled in a specific Medicaid plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, your parent must be enrolled in a Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO). If they have Medicaid but no MCO enrollment, call 1-800-283-4465 first.

faqs.question: Can my parent use this program if they live in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes, the program can provide assisted living support as part of the individualized care plan, depending on your parent's assessed needs.

programs.name: New Mexico Senior Farmers' Market Nutrition Program (SFMNP)
programs.tagline: Your parent can get $100 yearly toward fresh produce at local farmers' markets if they're 60+ and meet income limits.

localEntities.name: NM DOH WIC offices, local senior centers, AAA/AARP

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ (or 55+ if Native American) and has limited income, they can receive up to $100 per year to buy fresh fruits, vegetables, herbs, and honey at participating farmers' markets and roadside stands in New Mexico. This isn't cash — it comes as an electronic shopper card that works only for locally grown, unprocessed foods.

Because this program operates on a first-come, first-served basis with limited funding, applications typically open in mid-February and benefits are distributed in July until funds run out. Your parent must reapply every year, even if they qualified before.

The income limit is 185% of the federal poverty level, but if your parent already receives SNAP, TEFAP, or CSFP benefits, they automatically qualify without a separate income test.

summary: Income at or below 185% federal poverty level

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online, by phone, or at any WIC office or senior center — benefits are issued in July on a first-come basis.

steps.title: Apply when enrollment opens
steps.description: Applications typically open mid-February. Apply as early as possible since funding is limited.

steps.title: Provide income documentation
steps.description: Show proof of income or current participation in SNAP, TEFAP, or CSFP programs.

steps.title: Receive electronic card in July
steps.description: If approved, your parent gets an electronic shopper card loaded with up to $100 for the year.

steps.title: Shop at authorized locations
steps.description: Use the card only at participating farmers' markets and roadside stands for fresh, locally grown produce.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Benefits issued within July if approved
applicationGuide.waitlist: First-come, first-served — no formal waitlist if funds run out
applicationGuide.tip: Apply as soon as enrollment opens in February since funding is limited and distributed on a first-come basis.

categories.name: Age Verification
items: Any government-issued ID showing date of birth

categories.name: Income Proof
items: Current SNAP, TEFAP, or CSFP participation letter (automatically qualifies)

categories.name: Residency
items: New Mexico driver's license or ID
Utility bill with New Mexico address

contentSections.text: Apply early when enrollment opens in February. This program runs out of money every year and operates first-come, first-served — there's no waitlist if funds are exhausted.
Native Americans qualify at age 55, not 60. This age difference is easy to miss but could make your parent eligible sooner.

faqs.question: What if my parent already gets SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: If your parent receives SNAP, TEFAP, or CSFP benefits, they automatically qualify for SFMNP without needing to meet the income test separately. Just bring proof of current participation in one of these programs.

faqs.question: Can my Native American parent apply at age 58?
faqs.answer: Yes. Native Americans qualify at age 55 or older, while non-Native Americans must be 60 or older. This is a specific program rule that's often overlooked.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent qualified last year?
faqs.answer: They must reapply every single year, even if they qualified before. There's no automatic renewal — everyone starts fresh each February when applications open.

faqs.question: Where exactly can my parent shop with this card?
faqs.answer: Only at authorized farmers' markets and roadside stands where farmers grow their produce within New Mexico or within an 80-mile radius. Regular grocery stores don't accept these cards. Check the SFMNP locations page for participating vendors.

faqs.question: What if the $100 runs out before the year ends?
faqs.answer: The $100 is a one-time annual amount. Once your parent spends it, there's no refill until they reapply and qualify for the following year. It's designed to supplement, not replace, regular grocery shopping.

faqs.question: Can my parent buy anything besides fruits and vegetables?
faqs.answer: They can buy fresh fruits, vegetables, herbs, and honey — but only if these items are locally grown and unprocessed. No processed foods, plants for growing, or items from wholesale distributors.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.tagline: Part-time paid training positions for parents 55+ who need work experience to find permanent employment.

localEntities.name: NICOA (AZ, MN, NM, ND, OK, SD, WI)
Local SCSEP offices via state workforce boards.[2][3][4][5]

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and unemployed with limited income, SCSEP provides part-time paid training positions (typically 20 hours per week) at nonprofits and public agencies. Your parent earns modest wages while building job skills in roles like customer service, maintenance, teacher assistant, or food preparation. Because this is a training program, the goal is helping your parent transition to permanent unsubsidized employment within the community.

Your parent must have family income at or below 125% of the federal poverty level (roughly $19,000 annually for a single person, $25,500 for a couple in 2025—contact local offices for current 2026 limits). Priority goes to veterans and their spouses, people 65+, those with limited English or low literacy, rural residents, people with disabilities, and those who haven't found work through other employment services.

Funding can fluctuate, creating waitlists in some areas. Multiple organizations run the program in New Mexico, including the state Aging department, NICOA, and Goodwill, so availability varies by location.

summary: Family income at 125% federal poverty level
U.S. citizen or authorized to work
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or authorized to work in the U.S.

Priority given to veterans and qualifying spouses, age 65+, limited English proficiency or low literacy, rural residents, people with disabilities, those with low employment prospects, people who failed to find work through One-Stop employment services, homeless or at risk of homelessness

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local SCSEP office to complete an application—enrollment happens if eligible and no waitlist.

steps.title: Find your local provider
steps.description: Contact NM Aging & Long-Term Services Department, NICOA, or Goodwill NM depending on your area. Multiple organizations run SCSEP in different parts of the state.

steps.title: Complete application in person
steps.description: Bring proof of age, income, unemployment status, and work authorization. They'll assess your eligibility and priority status.

steps.title: Get matched to training position
steps.description: If accepted, you'll be placed in a part-time position at a nonprofit or public agency. A physical exam is offered after enrollment but not required to apply.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible depending on location and funding availability
applicationGuide.tip: Veterans, people 65+, and rural residents get priority—mention these qualifications when you apply.

contentSections.text: Funding fluctuations may cause program pauses or waitlists. Some areas experienced slowdowns in 2025. Contact multiple providers to find the shortest wait time.
This is training for permanent employment, not a permanent job itself. The program focuses on helping participants transition to unsubsidized work in the community.

categories.name: Identity & Work Authorization
items: Birth certificate or other proof of age
Social Security card or other proof of work authorization

categories.name: Financial
items: Recent pay stubs or unemployment benefits statement
Any other income documentation for household members

categories.name: Employment Status
items: Records of previous job search efforts if available

faqs.question: What kind of work will my parent do?
faqs.answer: Common positions include customer service, maintenance, teacher assistant, childcare support, food preparation, retail assistance, and shipping/receiving. The specific role depends on what's available at local nonprofits and public agencies when your parent enrolls.

faqs.question: How much will my parent earn?
faqs.answer: Participants typically work 20 hours per week and are paid modest wages directly by the state Aging department. The exact wage amount isn't specified in available sources—contact your local SCSEP office for current pay rates.

faqs.question: Can my parent stay in the program permanently?
faqs.answer: No. SCSEP is a training program designed to help participants find permanent unsubsidized employment. The program provides job development, resume building, and post-placement support to help your parent transition to regular employment.

faqs.question: What if our income is just over the 125% poverty limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits are strict at 125% of the federal poverty level. Contact your local office to confirm the exact dollar amounts for 2026, as these change annually. If over the limit, ask about other employment programs that may have higher income thresholds.

faqs.question: Which organization should we contact first?
faqs.answer: Try all three: NM Aging & Long-Term Services, NICOA, and Goodwill NM. They serve different areas and may have different wait times. NICOA has a focus on American Indian communities but serves all qualifying residents in their coverage areas.

faqs.question: Does my parent get priority if they're a veteran?
faqs.answer: Yes. Veterans and their qualifying spouses get priority, along with people 65+, those with limited English or low literacy, rural residents, people with disabilities, and those who haven't found work through other employment services.
